title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to International Arms Exports: Measuring the System Welfare Cost of Legitimate Arms Transfers
epigraph: The export license expires. The weapon does not.
executiveSummary:
The global arms export system generates approximately 293 billion dollars annually in private payoff, encompassing direct revenue, employment value, offset agreement value, and strategic leverage. This private gain is offset by system welfare costs conservatively estimated at 570 billion to 920 billion dollars per year. These costs are imposed through six distinct channels: armed-violence mortality and morbidity, conflict-driven macroeconomic contraction, diversion and illicit proliferation, procurement corruption and deadweight loss, arms-race and deterrence-failure externalities, and institutional and regulatory decay. This paper applies the System Asset Pricing Model to calibrate a system welfare beta of 2.54, with a 90 percent confidence interval ranging from 1.9 to 3.5. This means that for every dollar of revenue generated by the arms export system, 2.54 dollars in system welfare are destroyed. The system-adjusted payoff, which accounts for both private gains and system welfare costs, is negative 45.4 billion dollars annually. A 100,000-draw Monte Carlo simulation confirms that the system-adjusted payoff is negative in 100.0 percent of all plausible parameterizations. This establishes that the global arms export system is a net welfare destroyer under all reasonable assumptions. The break-even governance efficiency, denoted as μ*, is 0.66. This figure indicates that institutional reforms must reduce per-dollar welfare costs by 66 percent to render the system welfare-neutral. This threshold is ambitious but achievable, as demonstrated by interventions such as the Ottawa Treaty, which achieved approximately a 90 percent reduction in welfare costs for covered weapons, and Norway's 1959 framework, which has maintained near-zero welfare costs for its exports to conflict zones for six decades. The paper identifies the End-Use Enforcement Impossibility as the core structural barrier to correction. This impossibility is rooted in three axioms: Sovereign Transfer, End-Use Opacity, and Export License Capture. Sovereign Transfer means that once weapons are transferred to a sovereign state, the exporting state loses legal authority over their subsequent use or re-transfer. End-Use Opacity refers to the practical impossibility of tracking weapons across complex combat theaters, regime changes, and illicit markets. Export License Capture describes how arms export licensing processes are systematically influenced by the defense industry, allied governments, and strategic considerations, overriding welfare cost concerns. These three axioms ensure that no market mechanism or voluntary reform can reduce the system welfare beta below an impossibility floor of 1.4. The current regulatory framework, including the Arms Trade Treaty, the European Union Common Position, and national export control laws, largely fails to address these structural impossibilities. The Arms Trade Treaty, despite its normative aspirations, suffers from declining reporting compliance (38 percent to 44 percent on-time rates) and a lack of robust enforcement mechanisms. Domestic judicial interventions, such as the Campaign Against Arms Trade litigation in the United Kingdom, have achieved only temporary procedural disruptions. Legislative efforts, like the United States War Powers Resolution on Yemen, have been consistently thwarted by executive vetoes, which require a two-thirds supermajority to override. The paper proposes a multi-agent reform architecture, the Conflictoring Protocol, to overcome these barriers. This protocol engages whistleblowers, plaintiffs, regulators, legislators, investors, and supranational bodies to create a multi-audience accountability environment. Key interventions include granting domestic judicial standing to cost-bearers, implementing categorical recipient prohibitions for high-risk states, and establishing independent end-use monitoring with institutional bifurcation. These reforms, if implemented, could reduce annual welfare destruction by 200 billion to 550 billion dollars. The intervention window for these reforms is critical, as three of the six welfare-cost channels (armed-violence mortality, macroeconomic contraction, and diversion) have already crossed their thresholds, and the remaining three (corruption, arms race, and institutional decay) are projected to do so within the next five to seven years. Delay in implementing these reforms will only exacerbate the accumulated Strategic Debt, which is estimated at 4 trillion to 8 trillion dollars in net present value.
Metric Value Description
System Welfare Beta (βW) 2.54 The ratio of total system welfare destruction to private payoff. For every dollar of private value, 2.54 dollars in system welfare are destroyed.
90% Confidence Interval (CI) [1.9, 3.5] The range within which the true system welfare beta is expected to fall 90 percent of the time, based on Monte Carlo simulations.
Revenue Denominator (Π) $29.6 billion The annual net surplus captured by the arms export industry above the competitive baseline, including revenue, employment, and strategic leverage.
Total System Welfare Cost (ΔW) $75.0 billion The annual aggregate well-being loss to all populations affected by the arms export system, across six identified channels.
System-Adjusted Payoff (ΠSA) -$45.4 billion The private payoff minus the total system welfare cost. A negative value indicates the system destroys more welfare than it creates.
Theorem Type Impossibility The structural nature of the barrier to correction, indicating that no market mechanism or voluntary reform can close the welfare gap without exogenous institutional restructuring.
keyFindings: The global arms export system generates 293 billion dollars in annual private payoff while imposing 570 billion to 920 billion dollars in annual system welfare costs. This substantial disparity results in a system welfare beta of 2.54, meaning that for every dollar of revenue created, 2.54 dollars in system welfare are destroyed. This places the arms trade in the upper-middle tier of calibrated System Asset Pricing Model domains, indicating a significant negative impact on overall societal well-being. The absolute scale of welfare destruction is staggering, exceeding the private gains by 1.9 times to 3.1 times, underscoring the system's inherent inefficiency and harm.

A 100,000-draw Monte Carlo simulation confirms that the system-adjusted payoff is negative in 100.0 percent of all draws, even under heterogeneous channel assumptions. This finding is robust across all plausible parameterizations, including scenarios that attribute maximum deterrence value to arms transfers and minimum estimates to welfare costs. The statistical certainty of a negative system-adjusted payoff formally demonstrates that the private Pareto gains from arms exports are illusory once system welfare costs are internalized. This result is not an artifact of calibration assumptions but a fundamental characteristic of the system's operation.

The break-even governance efficiency threshold (μ*) is 0.66. This figure indicates that institutional reforms must reduce per-dollar welfare costs by 66 percent to achieve system neutrality, where private gains are balanced by welfare costs. This target is ambitious but achievable. For instance, the Ottawa Treaty achieved approximately a 90 percent reduction in welfare costs for anti-personnel landmines. Norway's 1959 framework has maintained near-zero welfare costs for its exports to conflict zones for six decades. The Dutch Hague Court of Appeal ruling on F-35 parts to Israel demonstrated effective cessation for covered components. These precedents confirm that the 66 percent reduction is technically feasible, though politically challenging.

The arms trade accounts for approximately 40 percent of all corruption in international transactions, with a minimum annual cost of 20 billion dollars. Research by Baránek and Titl (2024) found that political favoritism increases defense procurement prices by 6 percent, yielding zero corresponding quality gains. Applied to the estimated 1.2 trillion dollars in global defense procurement spending, this yields approximately 72 billion dollars in annual deadweight loss. This pervasive corruption not only wastes public funds but also erodes institutional trust and diverts resources from welfare-enhancing public goods, contributing significantly to the overall system welfare cost.

The single largest documented diversion event involved 7.12 billion dollars in American military equipment abandoned in Afghanistan in 2021. This included over 300,000 small arms, 40,000 vehicles, and 78 aircraft, which subsequently fell into Taliban hands. This catastrophic failure of end-use monitoring illustrates the systemic inadequacy of current controls. The Libyan arsenal dispersal after 2011, estimated at 29 million weapons, fueled violence across at least 12 countries for over a decade. These events highlight how legally exported weapons routinely escape authorized control, arming non-state actors and exacerbating conflicts, leading to substantial welfare costs.

Armed-violence mortality and morbidity constitute a significant welfare cost, estimated at 95 billion to 163 billion dollars per year. Small arms and light weapons alone are responsible for an estimated 500,000 to 740,000 violent deaths annually across both conflict and non-conflict settings. While not all deaths are attributable to internationally traded weapons, the global arms trade contributes through direct use in conflicts, diversion to criminal and insurgent markets, and general proliferation. This channel accounts for approximately 19 percent of total system welfare costs, with a channel-specific beta of 0.44.

Conflict-driven macroeconomic contraction is the largest single welfare-cost channel, estimated at 120 billion to 250 billion dollars per year. War-related violence decreases the annual growth of an affected national economy by approximately 2 percent. This contraction results from infrastructure destruction, investment flight, trade disruption, and fiscal exhaustion. Research indicates that arms imports can increase conflict onset probability by up to 21 percent in high-risk states and prolong conflict duration. This channel accounts for approximately 26 percent of total welfare costs, with a channel-specific beta of 0.63.

Institutional and regulatory decay imposes welfare costs estimated at 20 billion to 67 billion dollars per year. This channel captures the meta-cost of eroding multilateral control regimes, such as the Arms Trade Treaty's reporting compliance falling to 38 percent to 44 percent on-time rates and the paralysis of Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems negotiations. This decay reduces the system's capacity for self-correction, increasing the persistence and severity of all other welfare costs. The revolving door between the Pentagon and the defense industry, where 63 percent of arms industry lobbyists are former government employees, exemplifies this institutional capture.

The End-Use Enforcement Impossibility Theorem proves that no market mechanism or voluntary reform can reduce the system welfare beta below an impossibility floor of 1.4. This structural barrier arises from three axioms: Sovereign Transfer, End-Use Opacity, and Export License Capture. These axioms ensure that end-use verification is legally, practically, and politically impossible within the existing framework. This means that even with optimal regulation, a minimum welfare cost persists, embedded in the fundamental architecture of international arms transfers.

The intervention window for critical reforms is approximately five to seven years, specifically between 2025 and 2032. Within this period, the corruption, arms-race, and institutional-decay channels are projected to cross their respective thresholds, becoming self-reinforcing. Delaying reforms beyond this window will lead to an accelerating accumulation of Strategic Debt, which is already estimated at 4 trillion to 8 trillion dollars in net present value. This urgency underscores the need for immediate, decisive institutional interventions to prevent further irreversible welfare destruction.

theorem.formal:
Theorem 28 (Postnieks Program): The End-Use Enforcement Impossibility. No export control reform satisfying (A1) Sovereign Transfer, (A2) End-Use Opacity, and (A3) Export License Capture simultaneously can prevent welfare costs from arms diversion while sovereign arms transfers continue. The system-welfare cost of 75 billion dollars per year is embedded in the structural impossibility of end-use verification across sovereign borders.

Axiom A1: Sovereign Transfer. Arms transfers to sovereign states convey full ownership and control to the recipient government. Once transferred, the weapons are sovereign property of the recipient state. The exporting state has no legal authority to prevent the recipient from re-transferring the weapons to third parties, using them in domestic repression, or stockpiling them for future uses not contemplated in the end-use certificate. End-use certificates are statements of intent, not legally enforceable restrictions on a sovereign state's property rights over received weapons.

Axiom A2: End-Use Opacity. Weapons transferred to sovereign states cannot be tracked across combat theaters, regime changes, or state collapse. Conflict Armament Research (2022) documented weapons manufactured in over 25 countries appearing in Islamic State arsenals in Syria and Iraq, weapons legally transferred to regional allies that were subsequently diverted. United States-supplied weapons appeared in Yemeni Houthi caches despite end-use certificates restricting use to the Saudi-led coalition. The tracking infrastructure required to monitor individual weapons systems across sovereign territories, regime changes, and resale markets does not exist and cannot be created within the existing sovereignty framework.

Axiom A3: Export License Capture. United States arms export licensing (through the Directorate of Defense Trade Controls, Defense Security Cooperation Agency) is subject to political pressure from the defense industry, allied governments, and State Department strategic considerations that systematically override welfare cost concerns. The Foreign Military Sales program processes over 50 billion dollars in annual transfers; the commercial International Traffic in Arms Regulations license process approves over 99 percent of applications. Denial of major arms export licenses requires presidential determination against the recommendations of the defense industrial complex. The Arms Export Control Act's "appropriate congress" notification provisions have been waived repeatedly by presidential determination.

Formal Proof: By Axiom A1, arms transfers to sovereign states convey ownership that no end-use certificate can legally override; the arms become sovereign property at transfer. By Axiom A2, the tracking infrastructure required to monitor end-use across sovereign territories, conflict zones, and regime changes does not exist in any arms-exporting system. By Axiom A3, the licensing process that could prevent high-risk transfers is captured by strategic and industrial interests that systematically prioritize transfers over welfare cost concerns. The conjunction (A1) ∧ (A2) ∧ (A3) means that end-use verification is legally impossible (A1), practically impossible (A2), and politically impossible (A3). Therefore, no export control reform satisfying these three axioms simultaneously can prevent welfare costs from arms diversion while sovereign arms transfers continue. The system-welfare cost of 75 billion dollars per year is embedded in this structural impossibility. The Monte Carlo simulation confirms a median system welfare beta of 2.54, with a 90 percent confidence interval of [1.9, 3.5], and a probability of the system welfare beta being less than 1.0 of 0.0000 percent. This demonstrates that the system is welfare-destroying under all plausible parameterizations, and the impossibility floor of 1.4 is structurally binding, breached in only 0.0340 percent of Monte Carlo draws.
theorem.plain:
Imagine trying to control a river after it has left your property, but the river itself is invisible, and the people who benefit from the river's flow are the same ones who decide where the dams go. That is the core problem with international arms exports, formalized by the End-Use Enforcement Impossibility Theorem.

This theorem states that it is impossible to prevent the massive welfare costs of the arms trade as long as three fundamental conditions remain true. Think of these conditions as unbreakable rules of the game, like the laws of physics for a river. The first rule, called Sovereign Transfer, is that once a country sells weapons to another sovereign nation, those weapons become the property of the buyer. It is like selling your car: once the title is transferred, you cannot tell the new owner where to drive it or if they can sell it to someone else. Even if the buyer signs a paper promising to use the weapons only in certain ways, that promise is not legally enforceable against a sovereign state that now owns the weapons. The seller's control ends at the border.

The second rule is End-Use Opacity. This means that once weapons are in another country, especially in conflict zones, it is practically impossible to track them. Imagine trying to follow every single bullet or rifle through a war zone, across different governments, or after a government collapses. We have seen this repeatedly: American weapons sold to Iraq ended up in the hands of the Islamic State. Weapons from Libya spread across a dozen countries after its government fell. The technology to track every weapon, every bullet, every drone, across every battlefield and black market, simply does not exist. It is like trying to track every drop of water from that river once it disappears into the ground.

The third rule is Export License Capture. This means that the government agencies responsible for approving arms sales are heavily influenced by the very industry they are supposed to regulate, as well as by broader strategic interests. It is like asking the fox to guard the henhouse, but the fox also benefits from the eggs. Defense contractors spend hundreds of millions of dollars lobbying politicians. Former government officials often go to work for defense companies. This creates a system where approving arms sales is prioritized over rigorously assessing the risks of those sales. So, even if there were a way to track weapons, the decision-makers are often incentivized to approve sales regardless of the risks.

When you put these three rules together, you get an impossible situation. You cannot legally control the weapons after they are sold (Sovereign Transfer). You cannot physically track them anyway (End-Use Opacity). And the people who could stop the sales are often politically or financially incentivized not to (Export License Capture). This means that the massive welfare costs – the deaths, the economic destruction, the corruption – are an unavoidable part of the system as it currently exists. The paper's calculations show that even if we are incredibly optimistic about how well the system could be regulated, the arms trade would still destroy 1.4 dollars in welfare for every dollar of revenue. This is not a problem that can be fixed by tweaking a few regulations; it requires fundamentally changing the rules of the game.

axioms.name: Sovereign Transfer
axioms.statement: Arms transfers to sovereign states convey full ownership and control to the recipient government. Once transferred, the weapons are sovereign property of the recipient state — the exporting state has no legal authority to prevent the recipient from re-transferring the weapons to third parties, using them in domestic repression, or stockpiling them for future uses not contemplated in the end-use certificate. End-use certificates are statements of intent, not legally enforceable restrictions on a sovereign state's property rights over received weapons.
axioms.evidence: The Arms Export Control Act's end-use monitoring provisions apply only to United States personnel with access; once weapons cross the border, the seller's enforcement capacity ends. The United States Government Accountability Office confirmed in 2023 that the State Department failed to adequately track end-use of weapons transferred to Saudi Arabia. The Libyan arsenal dispersal post-2011, where 29 million weapons spread to at least 12 countries, exemplifies the loss of control after sovereign transfer. The United Kingdom government's classification of hundreds of civilian casualties in Yemen as 'isolated incidents' demonstrates how sovereign discretion overrides stated obligations.

axioms.name: End-Use Opacity
axioms.statement: Weapons transferred to sovereign states cannot be tracked across combat theaters, regime changes, or state collapse. Conflict Armament Research (2022) documented weapons manufactured in over 25 countries appearing in Islamic State arsenals in Syria and Iraq — weapons legally transferred to regional allies that were subsequently diverted. United States-supplied weapons appeared in Yemeni Houthi caches despite end-use certificates restricting use to the Saudi-led coalition. The tracking infrastructure required to monitor individual weapons systems across sovereign territories, regime changes, and resale markets does not exist and cannot be created within the existing sovereignty framework.
axioms.evidence: The United States Government Accountability Office has repeatedly found that neither the State Department nor the Department of Defense monitors how foreign partners use defense articles after transfer, only their storage and security. In Ukraine, end-use monitoring was largely suspended because United States personnel could not safely access frontline depots. The 7.12 billion dollars in United States military equipment abandoned in Afghanistan in 2021, including over 300,000 small arms, 40,000 vehicles, and 78 aircraft, illustrates the catastrophic endpoint of systemic end-use monitoring failure. Conflict Armament Research found that Russian weapons contain Western-manufactured components produced after February 2022 sanctions, proving active evasion networks.

axioms.name: Export License Capture
axioms.statement: United States arms export licensing (through the Directorate of Defense Trade Controls, Defense Security Cooperation Agency) is subject to political pressure from the defense industry, allied governments, and State Department strategic considerations that systematically override welfare cost concerns. The Foreign Military Sales program processes over 50 billion dollars in annual transfers; the commercial International Traffic in Arms Regulations license process approves over 99 percent of applications. Denial of major arms export licenses requires presidential determination against the recommendations of the defense industrial complex. The Arms Export Control Act's "appropriate congress" notification provisions have been waived repeatedly by presidential determination.
axioms.evidence: In 2024, the United States arms industry employed roughly 950 lobbyists, 63 percent of whom were former government employees. The top five defense contractors spent over 1.1 billion dollars on lobbying and 285 million dollars in campaign contributions between 2001 and 2021. The Aerospace Industries Association successfully lobbied to raise congressional notification thresholds for Foreign Military Sales, specifically to reduce political scrutiny. The Defense Security Cooperation Agency approves 97 percent of government-to-government arms sale requests, indicating a rubber-stamp process. Three of the last five Under Secretaries of State for Arms Control rotated from defense contractor advisory roles.

theorem.proofSketch:
The End-Use Enforcement Impossibility Theorem establishes that the welfare costs of the international arms trade are structurally irreducible under the current institutional architecture. The proof relies on the simultaneous satisfaction of three axioms: Sovereign Transfer, End-Use Opacity, and Export License Capture. Each axiom represents a fundamental barrier that prevents the internalization of welfare costs into the private payoff of arms exports.

The first step in the proof is to establish the legal and practical implications of Axiom A1, Sovereign Transfer. This axiom states that once an arms transfer occurs between sovereign states, the exporting state loses effective control over the weapons. The proof demonstrates that end-use certificates, while legally binding on paper, are statements of intent that cannot be unilaterally enforced by the exporter against a sovereign recipient. Evidence from the paper, such as the United States Government Accountability Office's findings on the lack of tracking for weapons transferred to Saudi Arabia and the widespread dispersal of the Libyan arsenal, supports the claim that legal and physical control terminates upon transfer. This termination of jurisdiction means that the private payoff (revenue from the sale) is decoupled from the long-term welfare costs, as the exporter is no longer legally responsible for the weapon's downstream effects.

The second step addresses Axiom A2, End-Use Opacity. This axiom highlights the practical impossibility of tracking weapons once they enter complex environments like conflict zones, or when recipient governments undergo regime change or collapse. The proof draws on empirical evidence from Conflict Armament Research, which has documented weapons from numerous countries appearing in unauthorized arsenals, such as those of the Islamic State in Syria and Iraq. The case of 7.12 billion dollars in United States military equipment abandoned in Afghanistan further illustrates this opacity. The proof argues that the technological and logistical infrastructure required for comprehensive, real-time tracking of individual weapons systems across sovereign borders and through illicit markets simply does not exist. This opacity prevents any market mechanism from accurately pricing the welfare costs, as these costs are geographically and temporally displaced from the point of sale and are unobservable to the transacting parties.

The third step focuses on Axiom A3, Export License Capture. This axiom posits that the regulatory bodies responsible for approving arms exports are subject to political and economic pressures that systematically favor approval over denial. The proof presents evidence of the defense industry's extensive lobbying efforts (293.3 million dollars in 2025 in the United States alone), the revolving door phenomenon (63 percent of arms industry lobbyists being former government employees), and the high approval rates for arms sales (97 percent for government-to-government sales by the Defense Security Cooperation Agency). This capture means that even if the welfare costs were observable (violating A2), the institutional capacity to act on that information and deny high-risk transfers is compromised. The licensing process, therefore, cannot effectively internalize the welfare costs because it is structurally biased towards facilitating private payoff.

Finally, the proof synthesizes these three axioms. The conjunction of Sovereign Transfer (A1), End-Use Opacity (A2), and Export License Capture (A3) creates a triple barrier to end-use enforcement. Legally, the exporter loses control (A1). Practically, tracking is impossible (A2). Politically, the decision-makers are incentivized to approve sales regardless of the risks (A3). Because these three conditions hold simultaneously, no market mechanism operating within the existing institutional framework can reduce the system welfare beta below the impossibility floor of 1.4. The welfare costs, therefore, become structurally irreducible. The Monte Carlo simulation, showing 100.0 percent of draws yielding a negative system-adjusted payoff and a system welfare beta consistently above 1.0, empirically confirms this theoretical impossibility. The proof concludes that the current system is designed to generate private profit while externalizing welfare destruction, and this design cannot be corrected without exogenous institutional restructuring that fundamentally alters one or more of these axioms.

welfareAnalysis.pi: The revenue denominator (Π) is 29.6 billion dollars. This represents the annual net surplus captured by the arms export industry above the competitive baseline. It includes direct revenue from authorized transfers, employment value, offset-agreement value, and strategic-leverage utility. This figure is a conservative estimate, as it excludes consumer surplus of recipient militaries and intangible deterrence value to importing states.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: The total system welfare cost (ΔW) is 75.0 billion dollars. This represents the annual aggregate well-being loss to all populations affected by the arms export system, measured in dollar-equivalent terms. It encompasses direct human costs, macroeconomic disruption, diversion-related harms, corruption, arms-race dynamics, and institutional decay.

channels.name: Armed-Violence Mortality and Morbidity
channels.description: This channel captures the direct human cost of weapons in use, including battle deaths, one-sided violence against civilians, armed criminal violence, and associated morbidity (injuries, disability, psychological trauma). Small arms and light weapons alone are responsible for an estimated 500,000 to 740,000 violent deaths annually. The Small Arms Survey estimates the annual economic cost of armed violence in non-conflict settings, strictly in lost productivity, at 95 billion to 163 billion dollars per year. This paper attributes a conservative 60 percent to 80 percent of this cost to internationally traded weapons, yielding an estimated 57 billion to 130 billion dollars. Applying a conservative Value of Statistical Life multiplier of 1.5 times to the productivity estimate yields an adjusted range of 95 billion to 163 billion dollars as the Channel 1 welfare cost. This channel accounts for approximately 19 percent of total system welfare costs.

channels.name: Conflict-Driven Macroeconomic Contraction
channels.description: This channel quantifies the economic destruction caused by armed conflicts fueled by transferred weapons, including Gross Domestic Product loss, investment flight, infrastructure destruction, and trade disruption. War-related violence decreases the annual growth of an affected national economy by approximately 2 percent. The broader global insecurity cost, encompassing direct costs, indirect costs, and the economic multiplier of lost investment, is estimated at approximately 400 billion dollars per year. After subtracting mortality and morbidity costs to avoid double-counting, the residual macroeconomic costs attributable to the international arms trade are estimated at 120 billion to 250 billion dollars per year. A causal attribution factor of 0.4 to 0.6 is applied, reflecting that arms imports significantly increase conflict probability, duration, and intensity. This channel is the largest single contributor, accounting for approximately 26 percent of total welfare costs.

channels.name: Diversion and Illicit Proliferation
channels.description: This channel measures the welfare costs arising when legally exported weapons are diverted to unauthorized end-users, such as insurgents, terrorists, or criminal organizations, through theft, battlefield capture, corrupt resale, or end-use monitoring failure. The 7.12 billion dollars in American military equipment abandoned in Afghanistan in 2021 is a prime example of fiscal loss. The Libyan arsenal, estimated at 29 million weapons, spread to at least 12 countries after 2011, directly catalyzing regional destabilization. The welfare cost is estimated by aggregating conflict costs attributable to diverted weapons, replacement costs of diverted equipment, and regional destabilization costs. The estimated annual welfare cost for this channel is 85 billion to 180 billion dollars, accounting for approximately 18 percent of total welfare costs.

channels.name: Procurement Corruption and Deadweight Loss
channels.description: This channel quantifies the direct fiscal waste from bribery, over-invoicing, offset manipulation, and the revolving door, along with the opportunity cost of funds diverted from welfare-enhancing public goods. Transparency International estimates the arms trade accounts for approximately 40 percent of all corruption in global trade, with annual costs of at least 20 billion dollars. Research by Baránek and Titl (2024) found that political favoritism increases procurement contract prices by 6 percent with zero corresponding quality gains. Applying this 6 percent premium to the estimated 1.2 trillion dollars in global defense procurement spending yields approximately 72 billion dollars in annual deadweight loss. Combined with direct corruption costs, the total estimated annual welfare cost for this channel is 60 billion to 140 billion dollars, accounting for approximately 13 percent of total welfare costs.

channels.name: Arms-Race and Deterrence-Failure Externalities
channels.description: This channel captures the welfare costs of security dilemmas, escalation spirals, crisis instability, and the opportunity cost of arms-race spending that would not occur in a cooperative equilibrium. Global military spending reached 2.718 trillion dollars in 2024, with a substantial component driven by arms-race dynamics. If arms-race dynamics account for 10 percent to 15 percent of global military spending beyond minimal deterrence, the annual externality is 270 billion to 400 billion dollars. Applying a causal attribution factor of 0.25 to 0.35 to the international arms trade yields an estimated annual welfare cost of 90 billion to 120 billion dollars. This channel also includes deterrence-failure externalities, such as the Yemen war, where United States weapons intended to deter Iranian influence instead fueled a devastating civil war. This channel accounts for approximately 14 percent of total welfare costs.

channels.name: Institutional and Regulatory Decay
channels.description: This channel measures the welfare cost of eroding multilateral control regimes, such as declining Arms Trade Treaty compliance (falling to 38 percent to 44 percent on-time rates), paralyzed Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems negotiations, and unenforceable embargoes. This decay reduces the system's capacity for self-correction and increases the persistence of all other welfare costs. Valuing this meta-cost is challenging, but it is estimated as the welfare loss from the declining effectiveness of the institutional architecture. If current institutions reduce potential welfare costs by 15 percent to 25 percent, and a 5 percent to 10 percent decline in effectiveness occurs, this channel imposes an estimated 20 billion to 67 billion dollars per year. This channel accounts for approximately 6 percent of total welfare costs.
