welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The Monte Carlo simulation involved 100,000 draws to account for the covariance structure of channel uncertainties and the non-normal distribution of several parameters. This extensive simulation provides a robust estimate of the system welfare beta and its confidence interval, confirming the statistical certainty of the paper's core findings.

For each draw, the revenue denominator (Π) was sampled from a uniform distribution ranging from 200 billion dollars to 400 billion dollars. This range spans from conservative estimates, focusing primarily on direct revenue, to more generous estimates that include employment, strategic leverage, and maximum offset value. Each of the six welfare-cost channels had its cost sampled from a triangular distribution. Specifically, armed-violence mortality and morbidity (δ1) was sampled from a triangular distribution with a minimum of 95 billion dollars, a mode of 129 billion dollars, and a maximum of 163 billion dollars. Conflict-driven macroeconomic contraction (δ2) was sampled from a triangular distribution with a minimum of 120 billion dollars, a mode of 185 billion dollars, and a maximum of 250 billion dollars. Diversion and illicit proliferation (δ3) was sampled from a triangular distribution with a minimum of 85 billion dollars, a mode of 130 billion dollars, and a maximum of 180 billion dollars. Procurement corruption and deadweight loss (δ4) was sampled from a triangular distribution with a minimum of 60 billion dollars, a mode of 95 billion dollars, and a maximum of 140 billion dollars. Arms-race and deterrence-failure externalities (δ5) was sampled from a triangular distribution with a minimum of 90 billion dollars, a mode of 105 billion dollars, and a maximum of 120 billion dollars. Institutional and regulatory decay (δ6) was sampled from a triangular distribution with a minimum of 20 billion dollars, a mode of 43 billion dollars, and a maximum of 67 billion dollars.

Cross-channel correlations were incorporated into the simulation. Channels 1 through 3 (mortality, macroeconomic contraction, and diversion) were assumed to be positively correlated with a pairwise correlation coefficient (ρ) of 0.5, reflecting that conflict increases all three simultaneously. Channels 4 through 6 (corruption, arms race, and institutional decay) were moderately correlated with a pairwise correlation coefficient of 0.3. A cross-group correlation coefficient of 0.2 was applied between the two groups of channels. This correlation structure was implemented using a Cholesky decomposition of the correlation matrix, ensuring that the draws accurately reflect the interdependencies between different welfare-cost channels.

The simulation results yielded a median system welfare beta of 2.54 and a mean system welfare beta of 2.59. The 90 percent confidence interval for the system welfare beta was [1.9, 3.5]. The mean system-adjusted payoff was negative 47.0 billion dollars. Crucially, the probability of the system-adjusted payoff being negative was 100.0 percent across all 100,000 draws. This means that under no plausible parameterization, within the specified ranges and distributions, did the arms export system generate more private value than it destroyed in system welfare.

Sensitivity analysis demonstrated the robustness of these findings. The system welfare beta remained above 1.0 even under extreme parameter perturbations, such as reducing all channel estimates by 30 percent simultaneously (yielding a system welfare beta of 1.64) or doubling the private payoff to 400 billion dollars (yielding a system welfare beta of 1.72). The classification as a welfare-destroying activity is invariant to all plausible parameter choices. The Monte Carlo distribution of the system welfare beta also showed low relative uncertainty, with a standard deviation of 0.49 and a coefficient of variation of 0.19. The 1st percentile of the distribution was 1.66, indicating that even the most optimistic 1 percent of draws resulted in a system welfare beta significantly greater than 1.0. The simulation was run with a fixed seed of 42 to ensure reproducibility across all System Asset Pricing Model papers, confirming that the results are not an artifact of random variation.

caseStudies.title: The 7.12 Billion Dollar Afghanistan Diversion
caseStudies.content:
The single largest documented diversion event occurred in Afghanistan in 2021, when 7.12 billion dollars in American military equipment fell into Taliban hands following the collapse of the Afghan National Defense and Security Forces. This catastrophic event was a direct consequence of systemic end-use monitoring failure and illustrates the endpoint of the diversion cascade. The Special Inspector General for Afghanistan Reconstruction and the Department of Defense published detailed inventories of the abandoned equipment, which included over 300,000 small arms, more than 40,000 vehicles, 78 aircraft, thousands of air-to-ground munitions, and highly sensitive technology such as night-vision goggles and biometric devices.

The welfare costs of this diversion are orders of magnitude larger than the equipment's book value. The Tehrik-e Taliban Pakistan, for instance, acquired United States night-vision equipment, giving them a significant advantage over Pakistani forces. Encrypted dark-market sales of this equipment to Al-Qaeda affiliates further exacerbated regional instability. The downstream effects include increased armed violence, prolonged conflicts, and enhanced capabilities for non-state actors that the weapons were originally intended to counter. This event directly contributed to the welfare cost in Channel 3 (Diversion and Illicit Proliferation) and had significant spillover effects on Channels 1 (Armed-Violence Mortality and Morbidity) and 2 (Conflict-Driven Macroeconomic Contraction).

This case study highlights the structural inadequacy of end-use monitoring programs. Despite repeated warnings from the Government Accountability Office, neither the State Department nor the Department of Defense effectively monitored how foreign partners used defense articles after transfer, focusing instead on storage and security. In conflict zones like Afghanistan, United States personnel could not safely conduct inspections, rendering monitoring efforts largely ineffective. The 7.12 billion dollar diversion is not merely a fiscal loss; it represents a massive, involuntary transfer of military capability to an adversary, with long-term consequences for regional security and human welfare that continue to materialize years after the event.

caseStudies.title: The Libyan Arsenal Dispersal (Post-2011)
caseStudies.content:
Following the North Atlantic Treaty Organization-supported overthrow of Muammar Gaddafi in 2011, the vast arsenals of the Libyan government, estimated at 29 million weapons, dispersed across North Africa and the Sahel. This event represents a critical phase transition in regional proliferation and a stark illustration of the long-term welfare costs of arms transfers in fragile states. United Nations Panels of Experts documented Libyan-origin weapons, including shoulder-fired surface-to-air missiles, anti-tank guided missiles, and small arms, appearing in Mali, Niger, Chad, Syria, and Gaza.

The dispersal directly catalyzed the Tuareg rebellion in Mali, the rise of Al-Qaeda in the Islamic Maghreb, and the broader destabilization of the Sahel region. The Mali rebellion alone cost an estimated 5 billion to 10 billion dollars in military operations, refugee support, and economic disruption. The welfare costs from this single diversion event have persisted for over a decade, fueling multiple conflicts, increasing armed violence mortality, and contributing to massive displacement across an entire subcontinent. This case demonstrates how legitimate transfers to a sovereign government can, under conditions of state collapse, transform into a pervasive source of regional insecurity and welfare destruction.

The Libyan arsenal dispersal contributed significantly to Channel 3 (Diversion and Illicit Proliferation) and had profound impacts on Channel 1 (Armed-Violence Mortality and Morbidity) and Channel 2 (Conflict-Driven Macroeconomic Contraction). It underscores the multi-generational and irreversible nature of some welfare costs, as a Pigouvian tax imposed at the moment of transfer could not capture the cascading effects that unfolded over many years. The event highlights the critical need for robust end-use monitoring and post-conflict weapon security, which are currently lacking in the international arms export system.

caseStudies.title: Procurement Corruption: The Airbus and BAE Systems Scandals
caseStudies.content:
The defense industry is disproportionately affected by corruption, accounting for approximately 40 percent of all corruption in international transactions. The Airbus and BAE Systems scandals provide canonical examples of how procurement corruption generates massive deadweight loss and erodes institutional integrity, contributing significantly to Channel 4 (Procurement Corruption and Deadweight Loss).

Airbus, a major aerospace and defense contractor, settled for a record 3.6 billion euros in 2020 for institutionalized bribery across more than 20 jurisdictions from 2004 to 2016. The settlement, divided among French, United Kingdom, and United States authorities, revealed a systematic pattern of using intermediaries to pay bribes to secure contracts. This direct fiscal waste from bribery inflates prices, reduces competition, and diverts public funds from welfare-enhancing sectors to inflated defense budgets. The cost of this corruption is borne by taxpayers globally, representing pure deadweight loss with zero corresponding quality gains in procured equipment.

Similarly, the BAE Systems Al-Yamamah deal with Saudi Arabia, which involved approximately 6 billion pounds in illicit commissions paid to a Saudi prince over more than a decade, illustrates the depth of political capture. The United Kingdom government, under Prime Minister Tony Blair, shut down the Serious Fraud Office investigation into the deal on 'national security' grounds before it could conclude. This direct instance of political capture overriding anti-corruption enforcement demonstrates how the revolving door and concentrated private benefits can subvert regulatory oversight. The BAE Systems case highlights not only the direct financial cost of corruption but also the institutional decay (Channel 6) that occurs when anti-corruption mechanisms are deliberately undermined by political interests. These cases collectively demonstrate that corruption is not an incidental feature but a structural component of the arms trade, imposing substantial welfare costs through fiscal waste and institutional erosion.

policyAnalysis.currentFramework:
The current regulatory and legal framework governing international arms exports is a complex, fragmented, and often contradictory tapestry of national laws, multilateral treaties, and informal arrangements. This framework is designed to balance national security interests, economic benefits, and human rights concerns, but in practice, it systematically prioritizes the former two at the expense of the latter. Several key statutes, regulations, and agencies have jurisdiction over arms transfers. In the United States, the Arms Export Control Act is the foundational statute, authorizing the President to control the import and export of defense articles and services. The Directorate of Defense Trade Controls within the State Department is responsible for implementing the International Traffic in Arms Regulations, which govern direct commercial sales. The Defense Security Cooperation Agency within the Department of Defense manages Foreign Military Sales, which are government-to-government transfers. The Leahy Laws, codified in various appropriations acts, prohibit United States assistance to foreign security force units that have committed a gross violation of human rights with impunity. However, these laws are often subject to waivers and are difficult to enforce at the unit level. Internationally, the Arms Trade Treaty, which entered into force in 2014, is the most comprehensive legally binding instrument. It requires State Parties to assess the risk that an arms transfer would contribute to genocide, crimes against humanity, war crimes, or serious violations of international humanitarian law or international human rights law, and to deny exports if an overriding risk exists. The European Union Common Position on Arms Exports (2008) sets eight mandatory criteria for export licensing for its member states, including respect for human rights and international humanitarian law. The Wassenaar Arrangement (1996) is a multilateral export control regime that aims to promote transparency and responsibility in transfers of conventional arms and dual-use goods and technologies. The United Nations Security Council can impose arms embargoes, but its effectiveness is often hampered by the veto power of its five permanent members, who are also among the largest arms exporters. Despite this array of instruments, the framework suffers from critical gaps. There is no supranational body with the authority to impose a global Pigouvian tax or enforce compliance across sovereign borders. The United Nations Security Council, the only body with binding authority, is paralyzed by the veto power of the United States, Russia, China, France, and the United Kingdom. National security exemptions often shield arms transfers from standard transparency and procurement oversight. The brokerage exemption in the Arms Export Control Act, for example, allows United States citizens to arrange foreign arms deals without a license, representing a significant loophole. The current framework is characterized by self-assessment, discretionary standards, and a lack of independent verification, all of which contribute to its systemic failures.
Statute/Regulation Jurisdiction Key Provisions Effectiveness/Limitations
Arms Export Control Act (AECA) United States Authorizes President to control defense exports; requires Congressional notification for major sales; end-use monitoring provisions. Congressional notification often symbolic (veto power); end-use monitoring focuses on storage, not use; brokerage exemption.
International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) United States (State Department) Governs direct commercial sales of defense articles and services; requires licenses. High approval rates (over 99%); subject to political pressure.
Leahy Laws United States Prohibits military assistance to foreign security force units with gross human rights violations. Difficult to enforce at unit level; waivers possible; does not address post-delivery diversion.
Arms Trade Treaty (ATT) International (118 State Parties) Requires risk assessment for IHL/IHRL violations; denies exports if 'overriding risk' exists. Relies on self-assessment; no independent verification; declining reporting compliance (38-44%); major exporters not party.
EU Common Position on Arms Exports European Union (Member States) Sets eight mandatory criteria for export licensing, including human rights. Criteria subject to national interpretation; enforcement varies by member state.
Wassenaar Arrangement Multilateral (42 Participating States) Promotes transparency and responsibility in transfers of conventional arms and dual-use goods. Voluntary regime; no legally binding obligations; limited scope for emerging technologies.
UN Security Council Embargoes International (UNSC) Imposes binding arms embargoes on specific states or non-state actors. Often violated; paralyzed by veto power of permanent members (major exporters).
policyAnalysis.failures:
The current regulatory framework for international arms exports systematically fails to prevent welfare destruction due to a confluence of structural, political, and practical limitations. These failures are not incidental but are embedded in the institutional design of the system, which prioritizes private payoff and strategic interests over system welfare.

One primary failure is regulatory capture, exemplified by the revolving door between government and the defense industry. In 2024, 63 percent of arms industry lobbyists in the United States were former government employees. This ensures that individuals who would design and implement regulations are often those who profit from their absence or weakness. The Aerospace Industries Association successfully lobbied to raise congressional notification thresholds for Foreign Military Sales, specifically to reduce political scrutiny. This direct influence on policy-making ensures that the regulatory apparatus is shaped to facilitate exports rather than constrain them, leading to a systemic bias towards approval.

Enforcement gaps are pervasive. End-use monitoring programs, such as the United States' Blue Lantern and Golden Sentry, are structurally inadequate. They are designed to verify physical security and accountability, not whether transferred weapons are used in accordance with international humanitarian law. Only 0.5 percent of export license applications receive physical checks, and of those, 44 percent reveal problems. In conflict zones, where diversion risk is highest, United States personnel often cannot safely conduct inspections. The 7.12 billion dollars in equipment abandoned in Afghanistan in 2021 is a stark testament to the failure of these monitoring efforts. The Arms Export Control Act also lacks a definition of 'civilian harm' or 'human rights violations' as 'misuse,' meaning these programs have no legal mandate to detect the most consequential forms of welfare destruction.

Jurisdictional arbitrage undermines international efforts. The Arms Trade Treaty, despite being signed by 118 State Parties, suffers from declining reporting compliance, which has fallen to 38 percent to 44 percent on-time rates. Major exporters like the United States, Russia, and China are either not party to the treaty or enforce it selectively. This allows states to exploit differences in national regulations, diverting transfers through less stringent jurisdictions. The United Nations Security Council, the only body with binding authority, is paralyzed by the veto power of its five permanent members, who are also the largest arms exporters. This structural conflict of interest ensures that any binding multilateral intervention that might genuinely constrain arms exports is effectively blocked.

The 'isolated incidents' doctrine, as demonstrated by the United Kingdom government's response to the Campaign Against Arms Trade litigation, illustrates how sovereign discretion converts binding obligations into bureaucratic formalities. After a court ruled the United Kingdom's Saudi export-licensing process unlawful, the government resumed licenses by classifying hundreds of civilian casualties as 'isolated incidents,' effectively sidestepping the intent of the ruling. This highlights the failure of discretionary standards, which are easily manipulated by states with strong economic and strategic incentives to continue exports.

Finally, the constitutional arithmetic of legislative oversight, particularly in the United States, ensures failure. The War Powers Resolution, which allows Congress to end military engagements, requires a two-thirds supermajority to override a presidential veto. This threshold is almost impossible to achieve in a polarized political environment, meaning the President effectively holds a unilateral veto over congressional attempts to block arms sales. This structural design ensures that even when there is bipartisan opposition to specific arms transfers, the executive branch can proceed with sales, further entrenching the system's welfare-destroying trajectory.
policyAnalysis.reform:
Reforming the international arms export system to mitigate its substantial welfare costs requires structural interventions that address the fundamental failures of the current framework. These reforms must overcome the institutional capture, enforcement gaps, and jurisdictional arbitrage that currently characterize the system. The paper identifies several proven solutions, drawing lessons from successful interventions in specific contexts.

One critical reform is the implementation of mandatory binding risk assessments with independent verification. This would convert the Arms Trade Treaty's self-assessment mechanism into a robust, externally audited process. Establishing an independent International Arms Trade Review Board, modeled on the International Atomic Energy Agency's safeguards system, would provide an impartial body to conduct risk assessments, publish findings, and refer violations to international bodies like the United Nations Security Council or the International Criminal Court. This would directly target Axiom A3 (Export License Capture) by introducing an external check on the captured licensing process. The estimated annual cost for such a board is 500 million dollars, with an expected 15 percent to 25 percent reduction in system welfare beta across Channels 1 through 3.

Another effective reform involves categorical recipient prohibitions, extending the successful model of the Ottawa Treaty from weapon types to recipient categories. This would prohibit all arms transfers to states scoring below a certain threshold (e.g., 30 out of 100) on Transparency International's Government Defence Integrity Index, absent a United Nations Security Council waiver. This bright-line rule removes the subjective-assessment vulnerability that allows governments to justify exports to high-risk states. This targets Axiom A3 by limiting the discretion that leads to capture. The Ottawa Treaty, which achieved approximately a 90 percent reduction in welfare costs for anti-personnel landmines, demonstrates the power of such categorical bans. This reform has an estimated implementation cost of 0.5 billion to 1 billion dollars annually, with an expected 10 percent to 20 percent reduction in system welfare beta across Channels 1, 3, and 4.

Domestic judicial standing for cost-bearers is a third crucial reform. Enacting legislation in exporting states that grants legal standing to affected civilian populations to challenge export licenses in domestic courts would empower those who bear the welfare costs. The Dutch Hague Court of Appeal ruling on F-35 parts to Israel, which halted exports due to a 'clear risk' of international humanitarian law violations, demonstrates the potential of domestic judicial enforcement, especially when aligned with political pressure. If Yemeni civilians could challenge United Kingdom export licenses to Saudi Arabia with similar standing, the 'isolated incidents' doctrine would likely not survive judicial scrutiny. This targets Axiom A1 (Sovereign Transfer) by introducing a legal mechanism to hold exporters accountable for downstream effects. This reform has an estimated implementation cost of 3 billion to 8 billion dollars annually, with an expected 10 percent to 15 percent reduction in system welfare beta across all channels.

Most jurisdictions have not adopted these proven solutions due to the formidable political economy of the arms trade. The 293 billion dollar per year concentrated private benefit, coupled with the lobbying power of defense contractors and the strategic interests of exporting governments, creates a powerful coalition resistant to reform. The United States, for example, has not ratified the Arms Trade Treaty, and its legislative efforts to block arms sales have been consistently thwarted by executive vetoes. The erosion of categorical bans, such as Lithuania's withdrawal from the Convention on Cluster Munitions, further illustrates the fragility of normative frameworks under geopolitical pressure. Overcoming this resistance requires sustained political will and a multi-agent approach, as outlined in the Conflictoring Protocol, to create sufficient external pressure for structural change.

agents.insider:
Insiders and whistleblowers within the defense industry, military, or export-licensing agencies play a critical role in exposing welfare-destroying conduct that is otherwise obscured by secrecy and institutional capture. These individuals possess unique access to information regarding violations of the Arms Export Control Act, the Leahy Laws, or the Arms Trade Treaty's prohibitions. The United States federal government provides several legally protected channels and financial incentives for such disclosures.

For United States federal employees and contractors, the Whistleblower Protection Act (5 U.S.C. §2302(b)(8)) protects those who report violations of law, gross mismanagement, or abuse of authority. The Department of Defense Inspector General Hotline (1-800-424-9098) offers a confidential avenue for reporting. The Intelligence Community Whistleblower Protection Act (50 U.S.C. §3033(k)(5)) extends protections to disclosures of classified information through the Inspector General of the Intelligence Community. These statutes ensure that individuals can report wrongdoing without fear of reprisal, thereby breaking Axiom A2 (End-Use Opacity) by making internal information visible.

The False Claims Act (31 U.S.C. §§3729–3733) provides a powerful mechanism for private individuals to sue on behalf of the government for fraud in defense contracts. These 'qui tam' provisions allow whistleblowers to recover 15 percent to 30 percent of any settlement or judgment. In fiscal year 2023, the Department of Justice recovered 2.68 billion dollars in False Claims Act cases, with defense procurement being a top category. For employees of publicly traded defense firms, the Securities and Exchange Commission whistleblower program offers 10 percent to 30 percent of sanctions exceeding 1 million dollars. These financial incentives are crucial for motivating disclosures in an industry where the stakes are high and retaliation can be severe.

Specific actionable intelligence that insiders can document includes evidence of end-use certificate fraud, unauthorized re-transfers by recipient governments, lobbying contacts between licensing officials and defense-contractor representatives, manipulation of dual-use goods classifications, and instances where risk assessments were directed to reach predetermined conclusions. For example, documenting how the United Kingdom government classified hundreds of civilian casualties in Yemen as 'isolated incidents' to justify continued arms sales would be critical. The cost of inaction is substantial: a single whistleblower disclosure preventing a 100 million dollar transfer to a high-diversion-risk recipient could avert an expected 254 million dollars in system welfare destruction, given a system welfare beta of 2.54.

In the United Kingdom, the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 protects employees who report wrongdoing to prescribed regulatory bodies, such as the Serious Fraud Office, Her Majesty's Revenue and Customs, and the Export Control Joint Unit. These mechanisms, while varying by jurisdiction, collectively aim to break the opacity that allows welfare costs to accumulate unchecked. The effectiveness of these protections and incentives is vital for transforming the arms export system from within, by making the hidden welfare costs visible and actionable.
agents.plaintiff:
Litigation pathways offer a crucial avenue for holding arms exporters accountable for the welfare costs of their products, thereby challenging Axiom A1 (Sovereign Transfer) by introducing expected liability into the transaction cost structure. While sovereign immunity often shields exporting states from direct international lawsuits, domestic legal systems can provide recourse, particularly through tort law and human rights litigation. The Dutch Hague Court of Appeal ruling in 2024, which ordered a halt to F-35 parts exports to Israel due to a 'clear risk' of international humanitarian law violations, demonstrates the potential of domestic judicial enforcement when plaintiffs successfully establish legal standing and causal links.

Causes of action for plaintiffs can include negligence, complicity in war crimes or crimes against humanity, and violations of national export control laws. For instance, non-governmental organizations and affected individuals could argue that an exporter's failure to conduct adequate due diligence or to prevent foreseeable diversion constitutes negligence, leading to direct harm. Discovery targets in such cases would include internal company documents related to risk assessments, end-user certificates, post-shipment verification reports, and communications with government licensing agencies. These documents are vital for establishing the exporter's knowledge of potential harms and their failure to mitigate those risks.

Precedent cases, such as the Campaign Against Arms Trade litigation against the United Kingdom government, illustrate both the potential and the limitations of this pathway. While the United Kingdom Court of Appeal ruled the government's Saudi export-licensing process 'irrational and therefore unlawful,' the government ultimately resumed sales by reclassifying violations as 'isolated incidents.' This highlights the need for robust legal frameworks that prevent executive discretion from overriding judicial findings. The Dutch case, however, showed that judicial rulings can be sustained by political will, particularly in coalition governments where civil society pressure can be amplified.

Likely damages in successful cases could include compensation for direct harm (e.g., medical costs, property damage, lost income), as well as punitive damages for egregious misconduct. The prospect of significant financial penalties creates a powerful incentive for defense contractors to internalize welfare costs. The False Claims Act in the United States, for example, allows private individuals to sue on behalf of the government for fraud in defense contracts, with whistleblowers recovering 15 percent to 30 percent of any settlement. This mechanism can lead to substantial recoveries, as seen in the 2.68 billion dollars recovered in fiscal year 2023. Expanding legal standing for cost-bearers in exporting states, as proposed in the reform architecture, would significantly strengthen this litigation pathway, making it more difficult for exporters to ignore the downstream consequences of their transfers and thereby contributing to a reduction in the system welfare beta.
agents.regulator:
Regulators, operating within national export-licensing authorities such as the Directorate of Defense Trade Controls and the Defense Security Cooperation Agency in the United States, or the European Union Common Position on Arms Exports, possess the formal authority to control arms transfers. However, the System Asset Pricing Model framework reveals a fundamental design flaw: these regulators are often tasked with monitoring 'storage' rather than 'use,' and their mandates are frequently subject to institutional capture, which undermines their effectiveness. The core challenge for regulators is to create an information architecture that makes system welfare (W) measurable where market pricing cannot.

Existing enforcement tools include export license approvals, denials, and conditions. The International Traffic in Arms Regulations process, for example, requires licenses for direct commercial sales. However, the gaps are significant. The Defense Security Cooperation Agency approves 97 percent of government-to-government arms sale requests, indicating a rubber-stamp process rather than rigorous oversight. End-use monitoring programs like Blue Lantern and Golden Sentry are limited in scope, covering only 0.5 percent of export license applications, and focusing on physical security rather than actual use in conflict. This means regulators are often blind to the most significant welfare costs.

The primary failure mode for regulators is institutional capture. The revolving door phenomenon, where former government officials transition to defense industry lobbying roles, creates conflicts of interest. The Aerospace Industries Association successfully lobbied to raise congressional notification thresholds for Foreign Military Sales, reducing regulatory scrutiny. This capture ensures that the regulatory apparatus is biased towards facilitating exports, thereby perpetuating Axiom A3 (Export License Capture).

To become effective, regulators must implement the Bifurcation Principle: separating licensing (pre-transfer risk assessment) from monitoring (post-transfer compliance verification). These functions should be performed by separate agencies with independent reporting lines to eliminate the bureaucratic incentive to classify violations as 'compliant.' Implementing enhanced end-use monitoring, including independent third-party verification, satellite-based asset tracking for major conventional weapons, and real-time serial-number databases linked to Conflict Armament Research's iTrace system, is crucial. The estimated cost of such enhanced monitoring is 2 billion to 5 billion dollars annually globally, a fraction of the 85 billion to 180 billion dollars in annual welfare costs from diversion alone. This represents a highly favorable cost-benefit ratio of 17-to-1 to 90-to-1, yet no government has fully committed to this investment. Regulators must advocate for these structural changes to move beyond cosmetic compliance and genuinely reduce the system welfare beta.
agents.legislator:
Legislators, holding the democratic mandate to rewrite the rules, are the decisive agents for transforming the arms export system from an impossibility domain into one amenable to correction. Only legislators can directly challenge Axiom A1 (Sovereign Transfer) by imposing new legal frameworks that redefine the terms of arms transfers, or Axiom A3 (Export License Capture) by restructuring regulatory oversight. The current legislative landscape, however, is often characterized by symbolic gestures that fail to overcome executive power and industry lobbying.

In the United States, congressional committees such as the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, the House Foreign Affairs Committee, the Senate Armed Services Committee, and the House Armed Services Committee have jurisdiction over arms sales. The Arms Export Control Act requires congressional notification for major sales, but the War Powers Resolution's requirement for a two-thirds supermajority to override a presidential veto renders legislative blocking attempts largely ineffective. For example, despite bipartisan support for ending United States support for the Saudi-led coalition in Yemen, President Trump's veto of the War Powers Resolution could not be overridden. This demonstrates that existing bills and mechanisms are insufficient against determined executive opposition.

Structural interventions are needed. Legislators must enact liability expansion, granting domestic judicial standing to cost-bearers, as demonstrated by the Dutch model. This would allow affected civilian populations to challenge export licenses in domestic courts, thereby introducing a legal check on executive discretion. They must also remove regulatory preemption, preventing national security exemptions from shielding arms transfers from standard transparency and procurement oversight. The proposed SAFEGUARD Act, which sought to mandate tracking of human-rights violations in end-use agreements, failed to pass, highlighting the political challenges.

Legislators can also implement categorical recipient prohibitions, banning arms transfers to states with poor human rights records or high corruption risks, similar to the Ottawa Treaty's ban on anti-personnel landmines. This would replace subjective risk assessments with bright-line rules, reducing the opportunities for capture. Furthermore, campaign finance reform, revolving-door restrictions, and mandatory lobbying disclosure are essential to weaken the influence of the defense industry on legislative processes. The current institutional capture ratio of 29:1 (lobbying expenditure to regulatory enforcement expenditure) far exceeds the 1.52 threshold required for endogenous reform, implying that exogenous legislative intervention is necessary. Legislators must leverage their power to fundamentally alter the rules of the game, rather than merely attempting to enforce existing, flawed ones, to achieve the necessary 66 percent reduction in per-dollar welfare costs.
agents.investor:
Investors, particularly institutional investors and those focused on Environmental, Social, and Governance criteria, are increasingly recognizing the financial exposure associated with the arms export system's substantial welfare costs. The system welfare beta of 2.54 positions arms exports as a high-risk activity, generating negative risk-adjusted welfare returns. This translates into significant stranded asset risk for companies heavily involved in the arms trade, as future regulatory tightening, litigation, and reputational damage can severely impact their valuations.

Portfolio screening criteria for investors should incorporate the system welfare beta as a key metric. Companies with high exposure to arms exports, especially those with significant sales to high-risk jurisdictions or those implicated in diversion and corruption scandals, face a growing likelihood of regulatory penalties, legal liabilities, and market devaluation. The paper highlights that the classification as a welfare-destroying activity (system welfare beta greater than 1.0) is invariant to all plausible parameter choices, meaning this risk is structural, not speculative. Investors should screen for companies that demonstrate verifiable compliance with Arms Trade Treaty criteria, implement independent third-party end-use monitoring, and maintain transparent agent management practices.

Stranded asset risk arises from the potential for future policy changes to render existing assets or business models economically unviable. As the intervention window (2025–2032) for critical reforms approaches, the likelihood of such policy changes increases. Companies that fail to adapt to these evolving regulatory and normative landscapes risk significant financial losses. The comparison with the coal industry, where equity declined 80 percent to 95 percent in real terms before mandatory regulation materialized, serves as a stark warning. Investors who continue to fund companies deeply embedded in the welfare-destroying aspects of the arms trade are essentially betting against a future where system welfare costs are internalized.

Companies most exposed include major defense contractors with substantial international sales, particularly those with a history of operating in jurisdictions with high corruption risk or weak governance. These firms face not only direct financial penalties from anti-corruption settlements (e.g., Airbus's 3.6 billion euro fine) but also reputational damage that can affect future contract eligibility and access to capital. Investors can exert pressure by demanding greater transparency on welfare costs, advocating for robust end-use monitoring, and engaging with companies to develop strategies for transitioning towards more welfare-neutral activities. By repricing the system welfare beta as a tangible financial risk, investors can contribute to breaking Axiom A1 (Sovereign Transfer) by making the expected private gain insufficient to justify the regulatory and liability exposure, thereby driving systemic change from the capital markets.
agents.supranational:
Supranational bodies and international treaties are crucial for addressing the global nature of the arms export system's welfare costs, particularly where jurisdictional arbitrage undermines unilateral national reforms. These entities can define property rights in global commons that no single sovereign can own, thereby preventing the race to the bottom that occurs when Axiom A3 (Export License Capture) operates across borders. However, the effectiveness of existing international frameworks is severely limited by structural flaws and the political realities of great-power competition.

The Arms Trade Treaty, which entered into force in 2014, is the most significant international framework. It established the first binding international standards for arms transfers, requiring risk assessments for human rights and international humanitarian law violations. While it works by creating normative architecture, its primary weakness is a lack of robust enforcement mechanisms. Reporting compliance has fallen to 38 percent to 44 percent on-time rates, and major exporters like the United States, Russia, and China have not ratified or signed the treaty. This means the treaty is honored primarily in its breach, failing to significantly reduce the system welfare beta (less than 5 percent reduction for covered weapons).

The United Nations Security Council can impose arms embargoes, but its effectiveness is severely hampered by the veto power of its five permanent members, who are also the world's largest arms exporters. This structural conflict of interest ensures that any binding multilateral intervention that might genuinely constrain arms exports is effectively blocked. The Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons Group of Governmental Experts on Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems, for example, has been paralyzed for a decade by major-power vetoes, preventing the development of binding controls on emerging technologies.

Other frameworks, like the Wassenaar Arrangement, are voluntary and lack legally binding obligations, making them susceptible to non-compliance. The Convention on Cluster Munitions and the Ottawa Treaty, which categorically ban specific weapon types, have proven highly effective, achieving approximately a 90 percent reduction in welfare costs for covered weapons at their peak. However, recent withdrawals, such as Lithuania's from the Convention on Cluster Munitions, demonstrate the fragility of these categorical bans under existential security pressure, indicating a regression of normative frameworks.

What is missing is a supranational body with independent enforcement authority, insulated from the political and economic interests of exporting states. Such a body, perhaps an independent International Arms Trade Review Board, could conduct binding risk assessments and refer violations to international courts, thereby directly challenging Axiom A3. Furthermore, international treaties need to move beyond self-assessment and incorporate robust, verifiable monitoring mechanisms. Without these structural changes, supranational efforts will continue to provide normative guidance without the teeth to enforce compliance, allowing the arms export system to continue its welfare-destroying trajectory.

crossDomainConnections.connection: The arms export system shares a critical structural similarity with Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances contamination, both classified as 'Hollow Wins' in the System Asset Pricing Model taxonomy, but they differ fundamentally in their impossibility class. PFAS contamination is a 'Molecular Hollow Win' (system welfare beta = 35.2), where the impossibility is chemical: the fluorine-carbon bonds are among the strongest in organic chemistry, making PFAS bioaccumulation irreversible on human timescales. The welfare costs are locked in by the molecular structure itself. In contrast, arms exports are a 'Sovereign Capture Hollow Win' (system welfare beta = 2.54), where the impossibility is institutional and political. The welfare costs of arms exports, such as mortality, macroeconomic contraction, and diversion, are entirely reversible through institutional intervention. If the weapons stop flowing, the conflicts eventually wind down, and corruption declines. This distinction highlights that while both systems generate private profit at immense system welfare cost, the barrier to correction for arms exports is a matter of political will and institutional design, not immutable physical laws. This shared mechanism of private profit extraction at system welfare cost, despite different impossibility types, allows for meta-analysis of institutional structures that produce high system welfare beta values.

The arms export system and agricultural monoculture both exhibit the 'moderate system welfare beta, massive private payoff' profile, making them particularly resistant to reform. Agricultural monoculture is an 'Evolutionary Hollow Win' with a system welfare beta of 8.6, where welfare costs, such as soil depletion and biodiversity loss, are delayed by decades and operate on biological timescales, making them resistant to rapid correction. The private payoff from monoculture is substantial, generating enough concentrated benefit to fund political infrastructure that perpetuates the system. Similarly, arms exports have a moderate system welfare beta of 2.54 but a massive private payoff of 293 billion dollars annually. This generates sufficient concentrated benefit to fund extensive lobbying efforts (293.3 million dollars in 2025 in the United States alone) and maintain institutional capture. Both domains demonstrate that the most politically persistent welfare-destroying activities are not those with the highest per-dollar destruction (which tend to be regulated due to obvious harm) but those with moderate per-dollar destruction and very high absolute private payoff. This shared mechanism of political persistence, driven by the scale of concentrated private benefits, leads to similar policy challenges where naive Pigouvian taxation and voluntary compliance fail, necessitating structural institutional changes like categorical bans or judicial enforcement.

The arms export system and Bitcoin mining, both classified as 'Hollow Wins,' share the characteristic of generating significant system welfare costs for private gain, but they differ in the nature of their impossibility and political economy. Bitcoin mining is a 'Thermodynamic Hollow Win' with a system welfare beta of 5.0, where the impossibility is physical: its proof-of-work consensus mechanism inherently requires massive energy expenditure proportional to its security, leading to substantial environmental welfare costs. The system's 'trustless architecture' eliminates the trusted intermediary that could internalize these costs, and 'jurisdictional arbitrage' allows miners to relocate to areas with the lowest regulatory burden. In contrast, arms exports are a 'Sovereign Capture Hollow Win' with a system welfare beta of 2.54, where the impossibility is institutional. The welfare costs are embedded in the political economy of sovereign states, which have a structural interest in maintaining the arms trade for strategic and economic reasons. While Bitcoin lacks this sovereign-capture feature, both domains face challenges from the inability to internalize externalities. The common mechanism is the structural absence of a party incentivized to internalize the full welfare cost, whether due to thermodynamic necessity (Bitcoin) or sovereign political economy (arms exports). This comparison highlights how different underlying mechanisms can lead to similar outcomes of welfare destruction, informing a broader understanding of systemic failures.

faq.q: The paper claims the arms trade is 'welfare-destroying.' Does this mean all arms exports should be banned, even to allies for legitimate defense?
faq.a: The paper's finding that the arms export system is 'welfare-destroying' (system welfare beta = 2.54) does not automatically imply a blanket ban on all arms exports. The System Asset Pricing Model measures the ratio of total system welfare costs to total private payoff. A system welfare beta greater than 1.0 means that, on aggregate, the system destroys more welfare than it creates in private value. This is a diagnostic, not a prescriptive, finding. The paper explicitly states that the cooperative baseline payoff (ΠC) is greater than zero, meaning a welfare-neutral arms transfer regime can still generate strictly positive private value. This cooperative baseline includes transfers to stable democracies with robust civilian oversight, verifiable end-use monitoring, and low diversion probability. Such transfers generate legitimate revenue, employment, and deterrence value without proportionate welfare destruction. The issue is not the existence of arms exports, but the current institutional architecture that allows for the systematic externalization of welfare costs. The paper identifies a break-even governance efficiency (μ*) of 0.66, meaning a 66 percent reduction in per-dollar welfare costs would bring the system to Pareto neutrality. This can be achieved through targeted reforms, such as categorical recipient prohibitions for high-risk states, enhanced end-use monitoring, and domestic judicial standing for cost-bearers, rather than a complete ban. The goal is to reform the system to ensure that private gains are not achieved at the expense of disproportionate system welfare destruction, allowing for legitimate defense transfers to continue under stringent, enforceable controls.

faq.q: The paper attributes a significant portion of welfare costs to 'arms-race externalities.' How can you quantify something as speculative as an 'arms race' or 'deterrence failure'?
faq.a:
The arms-race and deterrence-failure externalities (Channel 5) are indeed challenging to quantify precisely, as they involve counterfactual scenarios of what military spending or conflict outcomes would be in a cooperative equilibrium. However, the paper anchors its estimate in empirically observable trends and established academic literature. The 90 billion to 120 billion dollars annual cost for this channel is derived from a range-based approach. Global military spending reached 2.718 trillion dollars in 2024, a 9.4 percent real-term increase in a single year. The paper estimates that 10 percent to 15 percent of this global military spending, beyond what would be required for 'minimal deterrence,' is attributable to arms-race dynamics. This translates to 270 billion to 400 billion dollars in annual arms-race externality. A causal attribution factor of 0.25 to 0.35 is then applied to account for the share directly caused by international arms transfers, yielding the 90 billion to 120 billion dollar estimate.

Empirical evidence supports the existence of these dynamics. The post-Ukraine European spending surge (17 percent increase in 2024) includes an estimated 100 billion to 150 billion dollars above the pre-2022 baseline directly attributable to the Russian invasion and subsequent arms-race dynamics. Similarly, Middle Eastern spending increases (15 percent in 2024) include 30 billion to 50 billion dollars attributable to Iran-Saudi-Israel arms-race dynamics. Deterrence failure is illustrated by cases like the Yemen war, where United States weapons intended to deter Iranian influence instead fueled a devastating civil war. While the precise counterfactual of 'wars averted' is unobservable, the paper includes a generous deterrence-value component in the private payoff estimate (293 billion dollars) to account for this. The Monte Carlo simulation tests the full range of these parameters, and even under conservative assumptions, the system welfare beta remains significantly above 1.0, confirming that the arms-race externality, while difficult to measure, is a real and substantial component of the overall welfare cost and does not reverse the paper's core finding.

faq.q: The paper claims 'institutional capture' by the defense industry. Is this not an unfair accusation, implying corruption rather than legitimate lobbying?
faq.a:
The paper's claim of 'institutional capture' is not an accusation of illegal corruption in every instance, but rather a description of a structural feature of the arms export system, supported by extensive empirical evidence. The term refers to a situation where the regulatory apparatus is systematically influenced by the industry it is supposed to oversee, leading to outcomes that favor private interests over public welfare. This is a well-documented phenomenon in political economy.

The evidence for institutional capture is robust. In 2024, the United States arms industry employed roughly 950 lobbyists, 63 percent of whom were former government employees. The top five defense contractors spent over 1.1 billion dollars on lobbying and 285 million dollars in campaign contributions between 2001 and 2021. This level of financial and personnel investment in influencing policy creates a significant power imbalance. The paper notes that for every dollar spent on lobbying, these firms received 1,813 dollars in taxpayer-funded Pentagon contracts, indicating a substantial return on investment that goes beyond mere 'legitimate lobbying.'

Specific examples of capture include the Aerospace Industries Association successfully lobbying to raise congressional notification thresholds for Foreign Military Sales, which directly reduces political scrutiny of arms transfers. The Defense Security Cooperation Agency approves 97 percent of government-to-government arms sale requests, suggesting a process that prioritizes sales over rigorous risk assessment. Furthermore, the revolving door places former defense executives in senior government positions, blurring the lines between regulator and regulated. The United Kingdom government's decision to shut down a Serious Fraud Office investigation into the BAE Systems Al-Yamamah deal under 'national security' grounds, despite 6 billion pounds in evidence of illicit commissions, is a direct instance of political capture overriding anti-corruption enforcement.

This institutional capture is a key component of Axiom A3 (Export License Capture) in the End-Use Enforcement Impossibility Theorem. It demonstrates that the regulatory framework is structurally biased, preventing the endogenous emergence of mechanisms to internalize welfare costs. The paper's analysis is not about individual acts of corruption but about the systemic influence that prevents effective oversight and accountability, leading to a persistent welfare gap that cannot be closed without exogenous institutional restructuring.

methodology:
The paper's methodology applies the System Asset Pricing Model, developed in Postnieks (2026a), to the international arms export system. This framework provides a unified welfare-cost metric that integrates diverse impacts into a single, comparable ratio: the system welfare beta (βW). The research was conducted in several key steps, drawing on a wide array of data sources and employing robust calibration and simulation techniques.

**Data Sources:** The paper synthesizes data from five distinct disciplinary literatures: defense economics, arms transfers and conflict, export control and treaty effectiveness, defense corruption, and autonomous weapons and emerging technology. Key data sources include the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI) for military spending and arms transfer volumes, the Small Arms Survey for armed violence mortality and illicit trade estimates, the World Health Organization for morbidity data, the International Monetary Fund for macroeconomic impacts, Transparency International for corruption risk and costs (particularly the Government Defence Integrity Index), and various government accountability offices (e.g., United States Government Accountability Office, Special Inspector General for Afghanistan Reconstruction) for end-use monitoring failures and diversion events. Academic studies, such as those by Pamp et al. (2018) on conflict onset and Baránek and Titl (2024) on procurement corruption, provide crucial causal estimates.

**Calibration Methodology:** The calibration involved defining the system boundary, the baseline welfare stake (W0), and identifying six distinct welfare-cost channels: armed-violence mortality and morbidity, conflict-driven macroeconomic contraction, diversion and illicit proliferation, procurement corruption and deadweight loss, arms-race and deterrence-failure externalities, and institutional and regulatory decay. Each channel's annual welfare cost (δk) was estimated in 2024 United States dollars, with explicit uncertainty ranges. The revenue denominator (Π) of the arms export system, encompassing revenue, employment, offset value, and strategic leverage, was estimated at 293 billion dollars annually. The system welfare beta (βW) was then calculated as the ratio of total system welfare costs to total private payoff (Σδk / Π).

**Monte Carlo Simulation:** A 100,000-draw Monte Carlo simulation was performed to account for the covariance structure of channel uncertainties and the non-normal distribution of several parameters. For each draw, private payoff was sampled from a uniform distribution, and channel costs were sampled from triangular distributions, reflecting their estimated ranges and most likely values. Cross-channel correlations (e.g., ρ = 0.5 for conflict-related channels, ρ = 0.3 for others) were incorporated using a Cholesky decomposition. The simulation yielded the median βW, its 90 percent confidence interval, and the probability of the system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA) being negative. The simulation used a fixed seed of 42 for reproducibility across all System Asset Pricing Model papers.

**Limitations Acknowledged by the Author:** The paper explicitly acknowledges several limitations. Causal attribution for welfare costs (e.g., the share of mortality directly attributable to international arms transfers) is imprecise and relies on expert synthesis. The deterrence counterfactual (conflicts averted by arms transfers) is unobservable, though a generous deterrence value is included in the private payoff. Channel independence is an approximation, as channels interact through complex feedback loops, meaning the additive estimate of βW is likely conservative. The private payoff estimate is deliberately conservative, excluding some potential benefits to bias βW upward. Temporal alignment is imperfect for some delayed costs, and geographic aggregation masks significant heterogeneity in βW across different arms-trade corridors. The valuation of institutional decay is acknowledged as speculative, though its exclusion does not alter the core findings. Finally, the author acknowledges his disciplinary background in finance and welfare economics, noting that domain-specific nuances from international security specialists might be missed.

**What Would Change the Result:** The central claim (βW > 1, meaning the arms export system destroys more welfare than it creates) would be falsified only under an exceedingly improbable conjunction of conditions. This includes empirical demonstration that the deterrence value of arms exports exceeds 451 billion dollars per year, armed-violence mortality attributable to internationally traded weapons is below 100,000 deaths per year with low productivity costs, arms-trade corruption costs are below 10 billion dollars per year globally, the arms-race increment of global military spending is below 3 percent, and institutional controls reduce welfare costs by more than 50 percent. The Monte Carlo simulation confirms that even under the most optimistic 1 percent of draws, βW remains significantly above 1.0, and the probability of βW being less than 1.0 is 0.0000 percent. The result is structurally robust, not an artifact of calibration assumptions.

timeline.significance: Superpowers used arms exports as primary instruments of competition, flooding developing countries with weapons. Welfare costs were extreme, but politically justified by existential nuclear deterrence logic. The Private Pareto Theorem was embryonic, with welfare costs acknowledged but subordinated to survival calculations.

Norway implemented a stringent framework for arms exports, maintaining near-zero welfare costs for its exports to conflict zones for six decades. This demonstrates that a 66 percent reduction in per-dollar welfare costs is achievable with political will and robust institutional design.

timeline.event: Arms Export Control Act Enacted (United States)
timeline.significance: This foundational United States statute authorizes the President to control defense exports and requires congressional notification for major sales. However, it also includes a brokerage exemption, creating a significant loophole for arranging foreign arms deals without a license, contributing to end-use opacity.

Global military spending declined, and arms exports were partially constrained by emerging humanitarian norms. The Private Pareto Theorem was in remission, as welfare costs declined with the winding down of conflicts and partial securing of surplus weapons.

This multilateral export control regime aims to promote transparency and responsibility in transfers of conventional arms and dual-use goods. However, it is a voluntary regime with no legally binding obligations, limiting its effectiveness in constraining welfare costs.

timeline.event: Ottawa Treaty (Anti-Personnel Mine Ban Convention) Adopted
timeline.significance: This treaty completely prohibits anti-personnel landmines. It achieved approximately a 90 percent reduction in welfare costs for covered weapons, demonstrating the effectiveness of categorical bans in reducing system welfare beta. The Leahy Laws also codified in the United States, conditioning military assistance on human rights records.

The United States poured 1.5 trillion dollars in military equipment into Iraq and Afghanistan, subordinating end-use monitoring to operational urgency. The Private Pareto Theorem metastasized, with the Islamic State's seizure of Iraqi equipment in 2014 demonstrating the system's pathological endpoint.

timeline.event: EU Common Position on Arms Exports Adopted
timeline.significance: This set eight mandatory criteria for export licensing for European Union member states, including respect for human rights and international humanitarian law. While a step towards normative standards, its effectiveness is limited by national interpretation and varied enforcement.

The dispersal of 29 million weapons from Libya to at least 12 countries represented a categorical phase transition in regional proliferation. This event crossed the diversion crossover threshold, demonstrating how legally exported weapons can fuel conflicts for over a decade, contributing to massive welfare costs.

timeline.event: Arms Trade Treaty (ATT) Entered into Force
timeline.significance: The ATT established the first binding international standards for arms transfers, linking them to humanitarian criteria. While a normative breakthrough, its structural flaws (self-assessment, no independent verification, no enforcement) led to disillusionment, with compliance declining and minimal impact on major conflicts.

The Saudi-led coalition's intervention, supported by United States arms sales, led to the world's worst humanitarian crisis. This conflict demonstrated the failure of ATT signatories to prevent transfers contributing to international humanitarian law violations, highlighting the limits of discretionary standards.

timeline.event: CAAT v. Secretary of State (UK) Ruling
timeline.significance: The United Kingdom Court of Appeal ruled the government's Saudi export-licensing process 'irrational and therefore unlawful,' temporarily halting new licenses. However, the government resumed licensing by classifying violations as 'isolated incidents,' illustrating the limits of domestic judicial intervention when the executive retains discretionary authority.

7.12 billion dollars in United States military equipment fell into Taliban hands, representing the largest unintentional arms transfer in history. This event solidified the crossing of the diversion crossover threshold and demonstrated the catastrophic endpoint of systemic end-use monitoring failure.

The Russian invasion triggered the steepest military spending increase since the Cold War, with global military spending reaching 2.718 trillion dollars in 2024. This initiated a new phase of accelerating arms exports and arms-race dynamics, pushing the corruption, arms-race, and institutional-decay channels towards their crossover thresholds.

The Hague Court of Appeal ordered a halt on Dutch F-35 parts to Israel due to a 'clear risk' of international humanitarian law violations. The Dutch government maintained the ban even after being given judicial permission to reverse it, demonstrating the potential of domestic judicial enforcement when reinforced by political calculus.

timeline.event: Lithuania's Withdrawal from Convention on Cluster Munitions
timeline.significance: This withdrawal, along with announced Baltic withdrawals from the Ottawa Treaty, represents an unprecedented regression of categorical weapons bans. It reveals the fragility of normative frameworks under existential security pressure and contributes to institutional decay.

timeline.event: UNGA Resolution 79/62 on Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems
timeline.significance: The United Nations General Assembly adopted a resolution calling for a legally binding instrument on autonomous weapons systems. While non-binding, it marks a political threshold, but the historical timeline for such treaties suggests a long path to effective regulation, with major developers often resisting constraints.

timeline.event: Intervention Window for Corruption, Arms Race, and Institutional Decay Crossovers
timeline.significance: This period represents a critical five-to-seven-year window before the remaining three welfare-cost channels cross their thresholds and become self-reinforcing. Delaying reforms beyond this window will lead to an accelerating accumulation of Strategic Debt, making the 66 percent governance efficiency target increasingly unattainable.

literatureContext:
This paper applies the System Asset Pricing Model, developed in Postnieks (2026a), to the international arms export system, providing the first unified welfare-cost metric for this domain. It synthesizes and extends five distinct disciplinary literatures that have historically operated in parallel, each measuring a fragment of the arms trade's welfare impact without integrating them into a single, comparable framework.

**Defense Economics:** This literature, with foundational work by Benoit (1970s), the Feder-Ram model, and Alptekin-Levine (2012) meta-analysis, examines the macroeconomic effects of military spending. Recent updates from the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (2024–2025) document the structural shift to record global military budgets (2.718 trillion dollars in 2024). This paper extends this by quantifying the 'arms-race externality' (Channel 5) as a welfare cost, moving beyond traditional analyses of military spending's impact on Gross Domestic Product to assess its opportunity cost and destabilizing effects.

**Arms Transfers and Conflict:** Led by Pamp, Rudolph, Thurner et al. (2018) and Moore (2012), this literature uses instrumental-variable methods to estimate the causal effects of weapons imports on conflict onset, duration, and intensity. Fritz et al. (2022) and Mehltretter et al. (2023) provide recent advancements in forecasting conflict fatalities and understanding non-state actor capabilities. This paper integrates these findings into Channel 1 (Armed-Violence Mortality and Morbidity) and Channel 2 (Conflict-Driven Macroeconomic Contraction), attributing specific dollar costs to the conflict-inducing effects of arms transfers, a step beyond mere correlation or fatality counts.

**Export Control and Treaty Effectiveness:** This body of work evaluates instruments like the Arms Trade Treaty, the European Union Common Position, the Wassenaar Arrangement, and the Leahy Laws, documenting the gap between normative frameworks and actual compliance. This paper builds on this by quantifying Channel 6 (Institutional and Regulatory Decay), demonstrating how declining Arms Trade Treaty reporting compliance (38 percent to 44 percent on-time rates) and the paralysis of Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems negotiations impose measurable welfare costs. It also provides a comparative analysis of the effectiveness of categorical bans (Ottawa Treaty) versus discretionary standards (Arms Trade Treaty), finding that the former achieves significantly greater system welfare beta reduction.

**Defense Corruption:** Anchored by Gupta et al. (International Monetary Fund) and Transparency International's Government Defence Integrity Index, this literature quantifies procurement corruption and its institutional drivers. Baránek and Titl (2024) provide precise quantification of the 6 percent procurement price premium due to political favoritism. This paper integrates these findings into Channel 4 (Procurement Corruption and Deadweight Loss), providing a unified dollar estimate for the fiscal waste and institutional erosion caused by corruption in the arms trade, which Transparency International estimates accounts for approximately 40 percent of all corruption in international transactions.

**Autonomous Weapons and Emerging Technology:** This nascent literature, including Horowitz (2016, 2020) and Garcia (2015, 2023), examines the implications of Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems and Artificial Intelligence-assisted targeting. This paper connects these emerging technologies to the System Asset Pricing Model framework by arguing that they amplify every existing welfare channel while degrading existing governance mechanisms, accelerating the system welfare beta trajectory and raising the break-even governance efficiency threshold.

This paper differs from conventional analysis by providing a contemporaneous ratio of welfare costs to private payoffs, avoiding the discount-rate controversies that plague integrated assessment models in climate economics. It establishes that the arms export system is a negative-sum system, with a system-adjusted payoff that is negative in 100.0 percent of Monte Carlo draws. Key citations include the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute Yearbooks, Small Arms Survey reports, Transparency International's Government Defence Integrity Index, and academic works by Pamp et al., Moore, and Baránek and Titl. By integrating these diverse literatures, the paper offers a comprehensive, quantitative, and policy-actionable framework for understanding the true welfare cost of the global arms trade, positioning it within a broader taxonomy of 'Hollow Win' activities.
